content.text: A vector is an ordered list of numbers arranged in a single dimension — the rank-1 building block of all machine learning. Feature representations, word embeddings, model weight sets, and gradient signals are all vectors. Grasping vector notation, operations, and geometry is essential for reading ML papers, debugging shape errors, and understanding why dot products power everything from linear classifiers to attention mechanisms.
. By default a vector is a
— its elements are stacked vertically — and its transpose
is a row vector. Individual elements are denoted
is the number of components; also called the

In the tensor hierarchy, a vector is rank 1 — it has exactly one axis, indexed by a single integer. A scalar is rank 0 (no axes); a matrix is rank 2 (two axes). In NumPy and PyTorch, a vector has shape
— a 1-tuple — which is distinct from a shape-
Every supervised learning example is represented as a vector. A tabular data point with 50 features is a vector in
. A 28×28 grayscale MNIST image is typically flattened into a vector in
. A sentence transformed by a language model becomes a vector in

(a BERT-base embedding). The model's learned parameters for one layer are stored as weight vectors and bias vectors. The gradient computed during backpropagation is a vector with the same shape as the parameter vector it will update.
The central operation in ML inference — the linear transformation — is a dot product between a weight vector and a feature vector:

is passed through an activation function to produce a neuron's output. Every neuron in every layer computes exactly this operation. Understanding vectors is therefore inseparable from understanding neural networks.
— any collection of n numbers is a vector; the same data structure handles features, weights, gradients, and embeddings uniformly
— BLAS routines and GPU kernels operate on whole vectors at once, making operations on shape-
vectors orders of magnitude faster than element-wise Python loops
— vectors have magnitude and direction; this geometry makes concepts like cosine similarity, projection, and nearest-neighbour search meaningful
— vector space axioms guarantee that the operations used in ML (addition, scaling, inner product) compose predictably
— gradients are vectors in the same space as parameters, enabling the parameter update
— all vectors in a batch must have the same length; variable-length sequences (text, audio) require padding or packing strategies
— in very high-dimensional spaces, all pairwise distances become similar, degrading nearest-neighbour retrieval quality
— a flat vector loses positional relationships (e.g. adjacent pixels); 2D grids or sequence models are better suited for structured data

The two fundamental vector space operations are addition and scalar multiplication. Both are element-wise. Adding two feature vectors combines their information. Multiplying a vector by a scalar stretches or shrinks it without changing its direction (for positive scalars):

attrs.latex: \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} u_1 + v_1 \\ u_2 + v_2 \\ \vdots \\ u_n + v_n \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \alpha \mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha v_1 \\ \alpha v_2 \\ \vdots \\ \alpha v_n \end{pmatrix}

content.text: (inner product) maps two vectors to a scalar by summing element-wise products:
Geometrically, the dot product equals the product of the vectors' magnitudes times the cosine of the angle between them:

This identity is the foundation of cosine similarity — widely used to compare embeddings in retrieval systems and semantic search. When both vectors are unit-length (norm = 1), their dot product directly equals
measures the size (magnitude) of a vector. Three norms appear repeatedly in ML:
— the straight-line length of the vector; used in L2 regularization and Euclidean distance
— sum of absolute values; promotes sparsity in L1 regularization (Lasso)
— the maximum absolute component; used in gradient clipping and adversarial perturbation bounds
has L2 norm equal to 1. Normalizing a vector divides it by its norm, preserving direction but setting magnitude to 1:
In embedding retrieval systems, storing unit-normalized vectors means cosine similarity reduces to a plain dot product — the cheapest possible similarity computation.
Cosine similarity between two vectors measures how aligned their directions are, independent of their magnitudes:

The diagram below shows vectors from three angles: their mathematical column notation, their geometric interpretation as arrows in 2D space (with vector addition following the parallelogram rule), and their role in an ML classification pipeline (feature vector → dot product with weight vector → scalar score → probability).

attrs.alt: Vectors: mathematical column notation, geometric arrow view with addition, and ML pipeline role (feature vector → dot product → score)

content.text: Use a vector whenever a quantity has an ordered set of components that should be treated uniformly. Concrete use cases:
Feature representation of a single training example (tabular, image-flattened, text-embedded)
Learned weight vector of a single neuron or linear layer
Gradient of the loss with respect to a weight vector, used in the optimizer update
Bias terms: one scalar bias per output neuron, stored collectively as a bias vector
Embedding vector for a word, token, user, or item in a recommendation system

Use a matrix instead when you have a batch of vectors or a 2D parameter set (e.g. all neuron weight vectors stacked into a weight matrix). Use a higher-rank tensor when the data has more than one spatial or sequential axis.

import numpy as np

# Create vectors
u = np.array([2.0, 3.0, 1.0])   # shape (3,)
v = np.array([1.0, -1.0, 4.0])  # shape (3,)

# Addition and scalar multiplication
print(u + v)            # [3. 2. 5.]
print(2.5 * u)          # [5.  7.5  2.5]

# Dot product: two equivalent ways
print(np.dot(u, v))     # 2*1 + 3*(-1) + 1*4 = 3.0
print(u @ v)            # same, preferred in modern NumPy

# Norms
print(np.linalg.norm(u))        # L2: sqrt(4+9+1) ≈ 3.742
print(np.linalg.norm(u, ord=1)) # L1: 2+3+1 = 6
print(np.linalg.norm(u, ord=np.inf))  # L∞: max = 3

# Normalize to unit length
u_hat = u / np.linalg.norm(u)
print(np.linalg.norm(u_hat))    # 1.0

# Cosine similarity
cos_sim = np.dot(u, v) / (np.linalg.norm(u) * np.linalg.norm(v))
print(f"cosine similarity: {cos_sim:.4f}")

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

# Feature vector and weight vector
x = torch.tensor([0.5, 1.2, -0.3, 0.8], dtype=torch.float32)  # shape (4,)
w = torch.tensor([0.1, 0.4, -0.2, 0.6], dtype=torch.float32, requires_grad=True)
b = torch.tensor(0.1, requires_grad=True)  # scalar bias

# Linear neuron: dot product + bias
z = torch.dot(w, x) + b   # scalar output
print(f"z = {z.item():.4f}")

# Backprop: gradient of z w.r.t. w is just x
z.backward()
print(f"grad of w: {w.grad}")  # should equal x

# Norms via torch
print(torch.linalg.norm(x))           # L2
print(torch.linalg.norm(x, ord=1))    # L1

# Cosine similarity (F.cosine_similarity needs 2D tensors)
print(F.cosine_similarity(x.unsqueeze(0), w.detach().unsqueeze(0)))
Introductory: building a binary classifier from a weight vector

import numpy as np

def sigmoid(z):
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z))

# Weight vector and bias (randomly initialized)
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
w = rng.standard_normal(4)   # ℝ⁴ weight vector
b = 0.0                       # scalar bias

# A single input feature vector
x = np.array([1.5, -0.8, 0.3, 2.1])   # ℝ⁴

# Forward pass: dot product → scalar logit → probability
logit = np.dot(w, x) + b
prob = sigmoid(logit)
print(f"logit = {logit:.4f}")
print(f"predicted probability = {prob:.4f}")
Realistic: semantic search using cosine similarity

import numpy as np

def cosine_similarity(a, b):
    """Cosine similarity between two 1-D vectors."""
    return np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b) + 1e-10)

# Simulate embedding vectors (in practice these come from an embedding model)
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n_docs = 5
dim = 8
doc_embeddings = rng.standard_normal((n_docs, dim))
doc_embeddings /= np.linalg.norm(doc_embeddings, axis=1, keepdims=True)  # normalize

# Query vector (could represent a question's embedding)
query = rng.standard_normal(dim)
query /= np.linalg.norm(query)  # normalize

# Compute cosine similarity between query and all documents
# With normalized vectors this is just a matrix-vector product
scores = doc_embeddings @ query   # shape (n_docs,) — dot product for each doc
ranked_idx = np.argsort(scores)[::-1]

print("Document ranking by cosine similarity to query:")
for rank, idx in enumerate(ranked_idx):
    print(f"  Rank {rank+1}: doc {idx}  score={scores[idx]:.4f}")
In a production recommendation system for a video streaming platform, each video is encoded as an embedding vector in
by a deep learning model trained on watch history. Each user is represented by their own

vector, computed as a weighted average of the embedding vectors of recently watched videos. At serving time, retrieving the top-100 most relevant videos for a user reduces to finding the 100 video embedding vectors with the highest cosine similarity to the user vector — an approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search in a vector index containing millions of item vectors. The quality of the recommendations is entirely determined by how well these vectors encode preferences — higher-dimensional vectors capture more nuance but cost more memory and increase search latency.
1-D array is a vector; a shape-
array is a column matrix. Operations like
behave differently depending on which shape is passed.
Forgetting to normalize before cosine similarity
— raw dot products favor high-magnitude vectors. Normalizing both vectors first makes the dot product equal to cosine similarity, which compares direction rather than magnitude.
Using Python lists for vector operations
. Always use NumPy arrays or PyTorch tensors for numerical vector operations.
Mixing broadcasting with unexpected singleton dimensions
matrix broadcasts the vector across all 4 rows (correct). Adding a shape-
matrix broadcasts across all 3 columns per row (may be surprising).
Dividing by norm without adding epsilon
— if a zero vector enters normalization, the result is NaN. Guard with
after creation and before key operations to catch dimension mismatches early
for dot products in modern code —
is cleaner and works for both vectors and matrices
Normalize embedding vectors at index time rather than at query time — if documents are unit-normalized once, retrieval reduces to a pure dot product with no division
Keep feature vectors in consistent dtype (usually
) and on the same device (CPU or GPU) to avoid implicit conversions that silently move data
When computing distances across a large set of vectors, batch the operation into a matrix multiply rather than looping over vector pairs
The dot product between two vectors of dimension
multiplications and additions. Computing cosine similarity between a query and
— acceptable for small corpora but impractical for millions of documents. Solutions:
— algorithms like HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) and FAISS reduce search to
query vectors into a matrix and using
— PCA or product quantization can compress high-dimensional vectors to shorter representations for cheaper search, at the cost of approximation

import numpy as np
import time

dim = 256
n_docs = 100_000

# Unit-normalized document embeddings
docs = np.random.randn(n_docs, dim).astype(np.float32)
docs /= np.linalg.norm(docs, axis=1, keepdims=True)

query = np.random.randn(dim).astype(np.float32)
query /= np.linalg.norm(query)

# Fast: matrix-vector multiply, one BLAS call  O(N*dim)
start = time.perf_counter()
scores = docs @ query          # shape (n_docs,)
top_idx = np.argsort(scores)[-5:][::-1]
print(f"Matrix multiply: {(time.perf_counter()-start)*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"Top-5 indices: {top_idx}")
— the foundation for all vector math in Python
— efficient approximate nearest-neighbour library for millions of high-dimensional vectors; supports GPU acceleration
— produces sentence embedding vectors from text;
returns a matrix of embedding vectors, one row per input text
Weight matrices, batch of vectors, 2D data
Batched sequences, image volumes, multi-head attention
Vectors introduce the same numerical hazards as scalars but compounded by dimension. Key reliability concerns:
— padding tokens or dead neurons can produce all-zero vectors; dividing by their norm gives NaN. Check
— a weight gradient vector whose L2 norm is very large (common in RNNs) causes unstable updates. Apply gradient clipping per the global gradient norm.
with small L∞ norm to an input vector can change the model's prediction. This is the basis of adversarial examples in computer vision.

— a feature vector that falls far outside the training distribution (very high norm, components outside the expected range) will produce unreliable predictions. Input validation and normalization at system boundaries mitigate this.
Think of a vector as an arrow from the origin to a point in an

encodes relative proportions of its components. Two arrows that point in nearly the same direction are semantically similar — high cosine similarity. Arrows that are perpendicular (orthogonal) share nothing — dot product is zero. The ML weight vector
defines a direction in feature space that the model finds most predictive: the dot product
measures how much the input arrow aligns with the decision direction.
A vector is a rank-1 tensor — an ordered list of n scalars, denoted bold lowercase
Feature vectors, weight vectors, bias vectors, gradient vectors, and embeddings are all rank-1 tensors
is the fundamental operation in every linear layer and attention head
Cosine similarity measures directional alignment, independent of vector magnitude; normalize first to turn it into a dot product
L2 norm measures vector length; L1 promotes sparsity; L∞ bounds the maximum component
is a column matrix — they behave differently under broadcasting and matrix multiplication
Batch similarity search is best done with a matrix-vector multiply (one BLAS call) rather than a Python loop over individual dot products
